title: C# Type Casting
content:
### Type Casting in C#

Type casting is converting a variable from one data type to another. C# supports implicit and explicit casting.

### Implicit Casting (Automatic)

Safe conversion from smaller to larger type. No data loss.

```csharp
int myInt = 100;
double myDouble = myInt;  // int to double (implicit)
Console.WriteLine(myDouble);  // Output: 100
```

Implicit conversion chain: `byte` -> `short` -> `int` -> `long` -> `float` -> `double` -> `decimal`

### Explicit Casting (Manual)

Requires a cast operator `(type)`. Used when data loss may occur.

```csharp
double myDouble = 9.78;
int myInt = (int)myDouble;  // Truncates decimal part
Console.WriteLine(myInt);    // Output: 9
```

### Conversion Methods

| Method | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| `Convert.ToInt32()` | Converts to int | `int i = Convert.ToInt32("123");` |
| `Convert.ToDouble()` | Converts to double | `double d = Convert.ToDouble("3.14");` |
| `Convert.ToString()` | Converts to string | `string s = Convert.ToString(123);` |
| `int.Parse()` | Parses string to int | `int i = int.Parse("123");` |
| `int.TryParse()` | Safe parsing | `int.TryParse("123", out i);` |

### Parse vs TryParse

```csharp
// Parse throws exception on failure
int number = int.Parse("abc");  // Throws FormatException

// TryParse returns bool and uses out parameter
if (int.TryParse("abc", out int result))
{
    Console.WriteLine(result);
}
else
{
    Console.WriteLine("Invalid number");
}
```

### Type Conversion with `as` Operator

```csharp
object obj = "Hello";
string str = obj as string;  // Returns null if conversion fails
```

> **Key Point:** Implicit casting is safe and automatic. Explicit casting requires a cast operator and may lose data. Use `Convert` class for type conversions and `TryParse` for safe string-to-number parsing.

quiz.question: Which type of casting is automatic and safe in C#?
What happens when you explicitly cast 9.78 to int?
